largestFacilityDetails: Location: Atlanta, Georgia, Fairwater site

Cooling system: Advanced liquid cooling with almost zero operational water use
Water conservation: Uses almost no water for cooling and avoids traditional evaporative cooling losses
Power strategy: Selected for resilient utility power, targeting 4x9 availability at 3x9 cost

dataCenterRealityCheck: Largest Facility: DataBank South Lake Campus (MCI3)
Location: Lenexa, Johnson County (Kansas City suburb)
Status: Operational & Expanding (mature facility, gradual growth)

Kansas Data Center Portfolio: DataBank MCI3 South Lake (Lenexa): 12.75 MW, 66,000 sq ft (primary); DataBank MCI1 South Lake (Lenexa): 6 MW, 6,000 sq ft (legacy); DataBank MCI2 Pine Ridge (Overland Park): 2 MW, 12,000 sq ft (NOC hub); secondary colocation facilities: 2-3 MW
Total Kansas Capacity: 25-28 MW (small, stable market)
Grid usage: <1% of state peak (manageable, no stress)
Jobs lost to AI: 2,000-3,000 (service sector, gradual)
Job displacement: MINIMAL (no cliff, gradual attrition)
vulnerabilityFactors: Minimal/no crisis risks: Kansas has one of the lowest-impact data center profiles of all states analyzed.
Grid Capacity: Kansas peak demand is about 20-25 GW; data center demand is 25-28 MW, under 1% of state peak, with no stress and manageable 2-3% annual growth.
Power Supply: Multiple energy sources including nuclear, wind, and natural gas support reliable utility infrastructure through Kansas City Power & Light and competitive Midwest electricity rates.

Water Efficiency: Liquid cooling technology and 10-20M gallons/year of estimated use create negligible demand; facilities are spatially separated from agricultural water pressure and have adequate central US water access.
Workforce Integration: 100-150 permanent jobs, no construction boom, no unemployment cliff, and an enterprise colocation model with diverse customers keep employment stable.
Community Support: Kansas is business-friendly, Lenexa/Kansas City are supportive tech markets, no major NIMBY opposition is visible, and Silicon Prairie momentum is positive.
Environmental Compliance: Mature DataBank operations, no major regulatory issues, Tier III design, redundancy, reliability, and compliance-focused operations keep risk low.
needsToHappen: Maintenance only - no emergency action required.

Action 1: Continue DataBank expansion. Complete MCI3 expansion to the 12.75 MW target; DataBank expansion is company-funded, supported by major equity backing including AustralianSuper, with Kansas City remaining a secondary growth market.
Action 2: Monitor grid capacity through routine annual reviews with Kansas City Power & Light. Renewable energy can expand through normal market growth; no emergency upgrades are needed.

Action 3: Support regional tech growth through Silicon Prairie initiatives, existing workforce programs, and carrier-backed fiber backbone maintenance. This is modest economic development, not urgent crisis management.
Action 4: Maintain community partnerships through Kansas City business engagement, local hiring emphasis, and existing education/workforce support. Companies can handle most costs directly.
Action 5: Continue regional coordination across the Kansas/Missouri Kansas City market, including shared infrastructure optimization and utility-level power grid coordination.
Total state investment required: $0 for crisis prevention; company and utility funding can handle normal growth.
timeline: 2026: DataBank MCI3 expansion is on track toward 12.75 MW; Kansas market remains stable with 2-3% annual growth, adequate grid capacity, supportive community conditions, and zero crisis probability.
2027-2028: DataBank expansion complete across the three Kansas City facilities; Kansas sees secondary market growth, stable grid conditions, and 100-150 permanent jobs without disruption.
2029-2030: Kansas City becomes an established regional colocation hub with 25-30 MW of sustainable capacity, 150-200 permanent jobs, and thriving community conditions.
2031-2035: Kansas matures into a regional enterprise-focused data center hub with 30-40 MW of capacity, 200-300 permanent jobs, and resilience remaining at 8-9/10.

inaction: Kansas continues thriving with no crisis scenario even if the state takes no additional emergency action.
Established market: DataBank has operated since 2005+ with proven colocation operations.
Gradual growth: 2-3% annual expansion is manageable and does not overwhelm infrastructure.
Minimal infrastructure demands: Data center demand remains under 1% of the state grid.
Diversified customers: Enterprise colocation avoids single-tech-giant dependency.
Stable employment: No construction cliff and no boom-bust labor cycle.
Business-friendly environment and proven compliance operations keep risk low.
Result: Kansas has zero crisis probability; unlike Indiana, Illinois, or Texas-style explosive growth markets, Kansas does not need emergency intervention.
action: Proactive enhancement is optional, not required for crisis prevention.

Action 1: Silicon Prairie Marketing (2026-2027). Position Kansas City as a regional data center and tech hub, highlight central location, low disaster risk, and business-friendly conditions. Cost: $20-50M; potential outcome: $500M-1B additional investment.

Action 2: Fiber Backbone Expansion (2026-2030). Expand high-speed fiber connectivity across the Kansas City region to support next-generation colocation demand. Cost: $100-200M; outcome: regional competitive advantage.
Action 3: Workforce Development Excellence (2026-2030). Expand tech training programs to support DataBank and broader tech company hiring. Cost: $50-100M; outcome: 500+ trained workers.

Action 4: Regional Tech Ecosystem (2026-2030). Support startup incubators, venture capital access, and Kansas City innovation culture. Cost: $50-100M; outcome: stronger regional innovation hub status.

resilienceDashboard.costOfInaction: No crisis; opportunity cost of slower regional growth, about $500M-1B over 10 years
resilienceDashboard.recommendation: No emergency action required; optional acceleration can strengthen Kansas City Silicon Prairie positioning.
resilienceDashboard.mitigation: Potential returns include $500M-1B+ in additional investment, $50-100M/year in tax revenue by 2035, 200-300 additional jobs, and stronger regional prestige. The bottom line: Kansas action is for acceleration, not crisis prevention; the state thrives either way.

dataCenterRealityCheck: Largest Facility: Core Scientific Calvert City
Location: Calvert City, Marshall County (northwest Kentucky)
Status: Fully operational (opened 2019, transitioning to AI colocation)
Kentucky Data Center Market: About 28 commercial data centers across 15 operators
Largest: Core Scientific Calvert City (150 MW)
Under development: PowerHouse Louisville (402 MW, Phase 1 130 MW by Oct 2026)
Power cost: 12.15 cents/kWh, about 20% under US average
Jobs created: 150-200 permanent data center operations roles
Jobs lost to AI: 5,000-8,000 service sector roles, gradual

vulnerabilityFactors: Grid Capacity: Kentucky peak demand is about 30-35 GW; current data centers are about 200-250 MW, under 1%; with PowerHouse projected capacity reaches about 450-500 MW, still under 1.5%. Status: adequate, no stress.

Power Supply: TVA partnership gives Calvert City cheap, reliable power; multiple utilities include LG&E, KU, and Jackson Purchase Energy; energy mix remains coal-heavy at about 67% in 2024. Status: stable.
Water Efficiency: Current demand is about 30-50M gallons/year; PowerHouse uses liquid cooling; Ohio River region gives Kentucky abundant water. Status: excellent.
Workforce: 150-200 sustained permanent jobs, no major construction boom, and no unemployment cliff. Status: stable.

Community Support: Core Scientific is a positive brownfield redevelopment; PowerHouse Louisville has some noise, water, and environmental concerns; Louisville Metro Council is studying regulations proactively. Status: supportive with monitoring.
Environmental Compliance: No major issues, brownfield advantage, and existing infrastructure reduce wetland/site disturbance risks. Status: good.
needsToHappen: Maintenance only - no emergency action required.

Action 1: PowerHouse Louisville oversight in 2026. Monitor Phase 1 delivery at 130 MW by Oct 2026, ensure grid coordination with utilities, and address community concerns around noise, water, and environmental impact. Cost: $0 regulatory oversight, no state investment required.
Action 2: Routine water management. Confirm PowerHouse liquid cooling deployment, monitor summer demand, and keep water planning within company responsibility. Timeline: 2026-2027.
Action 3: Optional workforce development. Support local hiring at Core Scientific and PowerHouse through community college tech training programs. Cost: $20-50M optional.
Action 4: Ongoing utility coordination. Continue TVA partnership at Calvert City, coordinate LG&E/KU capacity planning for PowerHouse, and avoid emergency upgrades. Cost: $0 utility responsibility.
timeline: 2026: Core Scientific Calvert City operational at 150 MW and stable; PowerHouse Louisville Phase 1 breaking ground with 130 MW operational target by Oct 2026; grid stable; Louisville council monitoring regulations; no crisis.
2027-2028: PowerHouse Phase 1 online at 130 MW; Phase 2 under construction with another 100+ MW; Core Scientific stable and transitioning to AI colocation; grid adequate; good managed growth.

2029-2030: PowerHouse full buildout around 355 MW operational, approaching 402 MW capacity; Core Scientific expands future colocation phases; total Kentucky capacity 500-550 MW, about 1.5% of state; grid remains adequate.
2031-2035: Kentucky becomes a regional sustainable data center hub with 500-600 MW of manageable capacity, 300-400 permanent sustained jobs, and resilience at 7-8/10.

dataCenterRealityCheck: Current Status: Stable, minimal market with no hyperscale activity
Maine Data Center Portfolio: Regional colocation facilities in a small market
No hyperscale campuses operational or planned
Current capacity: About 50-75 MW estimated, small market
Market ranking: Regional Northeast US market, not national
Major operators: Regional carriers and small colocation companies
Jobs created: 30-50 permanent roles in small market operations
Jobs lost to AI: 2,000-3,000 service sector roles, gradual
vulnerabilityFactors: Excellent position with no crisis risks whatsoever.
Grid Capacity: Maine peak demand is about 8-10 GW; current data centers are about 50-75 MW, under 1%; no growth projects announced. Status: excellent with abundant capacity and no concerns.

Power Supply: Central Maine Power is the primary utility, with a diverse energy mix including hydroelectric, renewables, and natural gas. Competitive rates create a regional advantage. Status: excellent.
Water Availability: Maine is water-rich, with multiple rivers and lakes, no major competition, and hydroelectric generation advantages. Status: excellent.
Workforce: No construction boom, no unemployment cliff risk, and small operations-focused stable jobs. Status: excellent.
Community Support: Small but growing tech sector, no major data center opposition, and economic development focus. Status: supportive.
Environmental: No compliance issues because there are no major projects. Status: excellent.
needsToHappen: Action 1: Market monitoring on an ongoing basis. Track data center interest, support routine utility coordination, and keep costs at $0 for monitoring only.
Action 2: Optional growth support from 2026 onward. Position Maine as a Northeast Edge Hub, support fiber infrastructure, and develop tech workforce capacity. Cost: $20-30M optional growth support.
timeline: 2026: Maine remains stable as a small market with 50-75 MW; grid is excellent with no concerns; no action needed.
2027-2030: Potential modest growth to 75-150 MW if recruitment succeeds; status remains stable with no crisis.
2031-2035: Maine could become a small regional hub if growth occurs, with 150-250 MW projected and resilience at 8-9/10.

inaction: Maine remains excellent with zero risk even with no state action.
Minimal data center activity means no boom pressure.
Grid is abundant with 8-10 GW peak demand and only 50-75 MW current data center demand.
Water is abundant with hydroelectric advantages.
Power is affordable with regional advantage.
Result: Maine has zero crisis probability and is in an optimal stable, small-market position with no infrastructure crises.
action: Optional growth can help Maine capture Northeast market share.

Action 1: Northeast Data Center Hub Branding. Position Maine as Northeast Edge & Colocation Hub, highlighting water abundance, hydroelectric power, fiber routes, and northeast location. Target regional ISPs, CDNs, and colocation companies. Cost: $10-20M optional marketing.
Action 2: Fiber Infrastructure Investment. Improve Northeast corridor connectivity from Boston to Maine to Nova Scotia and build regional advantage. Cost: $30-50M public-private optional.
Action 3: Workforce Development. Build tech training programs through community colleges and create a pipeline before companies arrive. Cost: $10-20M optional.

resilienceDashboard.recommendation: No urgent action required; optional branding, fiber, and workforce investment can grow Maine as a Northeast edge hub.
resilienceDashboard.mitigation: Potential returns include $20-40M/year in tax revenue if projects are attracted, $500M-1B in additional investment, 100-200 permanent jobs, and stronger Northeast market positioning.

vulnerabilityFactors: Grid Capacity: Maryland peak demand is about 25-30 GW; current data centers are 300-400 MW, about 1-2%; growth projects add 200-400 MW from 2026-2030; projected 2030 total is 500-800 MW, about 2-3%. Status: adequate with manageable growth.
Power Supply: BGE and PEPCO serve the market with a diversified energy mix including nuclear, natural gas, and renewables. Status: stable.
Water Availability: Chesapeake Bay region and Potomac River provide abundant supply with no agricultural crisis conditions. Status: excellent.
Workforce: Washington DC metro spillover provides tech talent; no construction boom or unemployment cliff risk. Status: good.
Community Support: Economic development focus, DC metro tech-friendly culture, and manageable NIMBY concerns. Status: supportive.
Environmental: No major compliance issues, but Chesapeake Bay protection requires environmental consciousness. Status: good.
needsToHappen: Proactive management and strategic growth support.

Action 1: Grid capacity planning in 2026-2027. Coordinate BGE, PEPCO, and utilities through unified planning, monitor 500-800 MW growth projection, and expand renewable energy toward 30%+ by 2030. Cost: $500M-1B, primarily utility responsibility with some state support.
Action 2: Workforce development from 2026 onward. Build community college tech training, partner with operators, and target 300-500 trained workers. Cost: $30-50M state + private.
Action 3: Water sustainability ongoing. Require recycling systems at 50%+ efficiency, protect Chesapeake Bay, and maintain monitoring and oversight. Cost: $100-200M developer cost-share.
Action 4: Regional coordination from 2026 onward. Coordinate Maryland, Virginia, and DC to prevent redundant investment and plan shared infrastructure. Cost: $0 coordination.
Total optional state investment: $600M-1.2B for enhancement.
timeline: 2026: Maryland stable with moderate growth at 300-400 MW; grid adequate with no concerns; no emergency.
2027-2028: Growth projects bring 100-200 MW under construction; utilities plan ahead and grid remains adequate; managed growth.
2029-2030: Total capacity reaches 500-800 MW operational; upgraded grid capacity remains adequate; jobs reach 300-400 permanent; thriving and sustainable.
2031-2035: Maryland becomes a regional DC metro data center hub with 600-1,000 MW manageable capacity and resilience at 7-8/10.

Timeline: 2022 construction began in September; 2023 ELN02 groundbreaking (100 MW HPC, 342,000 sq ft); 2025 CoreWeave 250 MW lease announced in June; 2025 $375M financing closed in February; 2026 ELN02 operational (100 MW); 2026-2027 ELN03 expansion and additional capacity scaling

CoreWeave Lease Agreement: 250 MW critical IT load (15-year term); ~$7 billion total revenue over 15-year contract; 150 MW additional option; AI/HPC infrastructure focus; long-term anchor tenant commitment

Infrastructure: Liquid-cooled infrastructure (ultra-high density GPU); purpose-built for AI/HPC workloads; generative AI, NLP, machine learning, and rendering focus; ultralow-cost, highly efficient design; rapid deployment capabilities

Strategic Position: Near wind farm substation (renewable energy advantage); rural North Dakota with abundant land and low population density; pro-business climate and economic development incentives; one of most ambitious data center developments in North America; designed to scale to 1 GW+ eventually

Economic Impact: Small town revitalization for Ellendale (1,100 population); 350+ job creation potential; 500-700 resident population growth expected; tax base expansion; housing development and workforce housing needed
Macquarie Partnership: $5 billion capital commitment (January 2025); senior secured note financing; strategic equity partnership for AI infrastructure expansion

Related Project: Polaris Forge 2 (Harwood, ND) - $3 billion investment; 280 MW capacity with scaling capability; groundbreaking September 2025; initial capacity 2026 and full capacity early 2027; advanced negotiations with U.S. investment-grade hyperscaler
Competitive Advantages: Wind farm proximity; rural location with lower operational costs; abundant available land; skilled workforce availability; state economic development support

Notes: Applied Digital's flagship Polaris Forge 1 Ellendale campus. 530 MW potential across three buildings (ELN01: 180 MW crypto, ELN02: 100 MW HPC, ELN03: 250 MW expansion). One of most ambitious North American data center developments. CoreWeave anchor tenant (250 MW, $7B/15 years). $375M SMBC financing (Feb 2026). Strategic wind farm proximity. Rural revitalization model transforming Ellendale from shrinking town to data center hub. Macquarie $5B partnership backing expansion. Polaris Forge 2 (Harwood, $3B, 280 MW) represents rapid growth phase. Designed to eventually scale to 1 GW+. Represents rural AI infrastructure model with renewable energy integration.

additionalProjectDetails: North Dakota Data Center Boom Summary: Applied Digital Polaris Forge 1 in Ellendale totals 530 MW potential with CoreWeave as a 250 MW anchor tenant on a 15-year, ~$7B contract.

Under Construction / Pipeline: Polaris Forge 2 Harwood ($3B, 280 MW), Polaris Forge 1 Expansion ($500M+, 300 MW), Critical Data House Williston ($5B, 455 MW), and Project Trinity Williams County (100 MW).
Total Pipeline: 1,665+ MW capacity, extraordinary scale for a rural state.

Strategic Advantages: Renewable wind energy, stranded low-cost energy resources, abundant land, pro-business climate, Macquarie $5B partnership backing Applied Digital, and Fargo regional connectivity.

Economic Impact: Ellendale 350+ jobs and 500-700 resident population growth; Harwood regional job creation and tax-base expansion; Williston economic diversification from oil/gas toward digital infrastructure.

Key Differentiator: North Dakota data center growth is powered by renewable wind energy plus stranded low-cost power resources, creating a cost-competitive advantage against states facing grid constraints and higher utility costs.

Prometheus: Gigawatt-scale supercluster (under construction)

Power capacity: Current: ~200-400 MW estimated; Prometheus: 1,000 MW (1 GW) - world's first gigawatt-capable facility; supporting power plant: 200 MW natural gas power plant approved March 2026 with completion Q3 2026
Power source: Primary grid AES Ohio; 100% renewable energy goal with two new renewable energy projects in Ohio; 200 MW dedicated natural gas power plant to support Prometheus
Status: Operational (Buildings 1-5 operational); Prometheus supercluster under construction (2026 operational target); continuously expanding
Investment: Original 2017 announcement of $750 million; cumulative to 2026 multi-billion dollar commitment; Corning partnership $6 billion fiber optic cable supply agreement (Feb 2026)
Employment: Hundreds of permanent jobs plus 25,000 construction workers on New Albany Business Park

Sustainability: LEED Gold certification for operational buildings; energy efficiency focus; advanced water-stewardship and cooling technologies; 100% renewable energy goal plus two new renewable projects
Community Investment: Educational partnerships (STEM programs, BizTowns); workforce development and training; local nonprofit and school support; Meta Quest XR education initiatives

Timeline: 2017 first Meta announcement and groundbreaking; 2021 Building 3 completion; 2022 Buildings 4 & 5 completion; March 2026 200 MW natural gas power plant approved; Q3 2026 natural gas power plant completion; 2026 Prometheus gigawatt supercluster operational target; continuous expansion ongoing

Prometheus Supercluster: Located at 1500 Beech Road, New Albany; 1,000 MW (1 GW) power capacity; world's first gigawatt-capable data center; 2026 completion announced by CEO Mark Zuckerberg; purpose-built next-generation AI supercluster; capacity could power 876,000 households for one year; novel highest-capacity supercluster architecture; Meta positioned first among Google and OpenAI gigawatt-capable plans
Supporting Infrastructure: 200 MW natural gas power plant approved with Q3 2026 completion; direct power feed to Prometheus facility; advanced cooling systems; water conservation technologies

Modular Construction: Giant tent-like structures with durable fabric over frames for Buildings 4 & 5; faster deployment than traditional warehouses; weatherproof, scalable design; AI-optimized architecture

Regional Context: New Albany International Business Park: 12,000 acres, 26,000 employees; Meta catalyst as first data center in 2017; current landscape of 40 operational data centers and 28 under construction/planning; major tenants include Meta, AWS, Google, Microsoft, EdgeConneX, and others; Columbus metro has the second-most hyperscaler-owned data centers in the US
Corning Partnership: $6 billion fiber optic cable supply agreement (February 2026); multi-year partnership; supports Meta data center innovation; benefits New Albany and central Ohio projects

Economic Impact: Data center investment larger than consumer spending in regional economy; tech hub catalyst transforming New Albany from exurban community to data center epicenter; Les Wexner / New Albany Company real estate value tied to AI infrastructure investments

Notes: Meta's flagship Ohio data center campus and world's first gigawatt-capable facility. 2.5M sq ft current campus expanding to 3+ million sq ft with Prometheus. 1,000 MW Prometheus supercluster operationalizing 2026. 200 MW supporting natural gas power plant approved with Q3 2026 completion. Catalyst for Ohio data center boom. LEED Gold certified with 100% renewable energy goal plus two new renewable projects. $6B Corning fiber partnership. Meta's largest infrastructure investment in Ohio. Continuous phased expansion with modular tent-like structures enabling rapid deployment. Prometheus represents next-generation AI supercluster architecture optimized for massive LLM training and inference workloads.

Notes: Meta Prineville represents flagship pioneering data center. First hyperscale greenfield development (2010 groundbreaking, 2011 operational). 4.6M sq ft ultimate from original 600,000 sq ft. $2B+ investment. Pioneering energy efficiency with 1.06 PUE. LEED Gold certified. Airside economizer, evaporative cooling, and MeeFog system enable year-round ambient cooling. Open Compute Project foundation. 350+ permanent jobs and 1,000+ peak construction workers. 100% renewable energy. Innovative ASR water partnership with city. Continuous expansion since 2011. Model facility for sustainable data center design and innovation.

largestFacilityDetails: Location: Salem Township, Luzerne County, Pennsylvania (adjacent to Susquehanna Steam Electric Station)
Acquisition date: 2024 (purchased from Talen Energy)
Purchase price: $650 million ($350 million at close, $300 million in escrow released upon 2024 development milestones)
Seller: Talen Energy Corporation (Cumulus Data division)
Original build: Talen announced July 2021; construction began 2021; Phase 1 completed early 2023 (300,000 sq ft hyperscale facility)
Land: 1,200 acres
Total space: Phase 1 completed: 300,000 sq ft hyperscale data center; planned expansion: 960 MW capacity campus
Configuration: Multi-building hyperscale data center campus (nuclear-powered)
Tenancy: AWS internal primary tenant (910-960 MW capacity); TeraWulf Nautilus cryptomining (50 MW existing); potential regional Pennsylvania delivery

Power capacity: Phase 1 operational: 300 MW; AWS planned maximum potential: 960 MW; AWS minimum contractual 120 MW increments ramping to 960 MW; one-time cap option of 480 MW; TeraWulf Nautilus 50 MW; Susquehanna plant capacity 2,520 MW total (two 1,260 MW reactors)
Power source: 100% carbon-free nuclear energy from Susquehanna Steam Electric Station; direct power feed from nuclear plant to AWS campus; two 1,260 MW reactors commissioned 1983

Power Purchase Agreement: June 2025 17-year PPA signed; 1.92 GW (1,920 MW) total supply capacity; effective until 2042; fixed-price terms; ramps up over 7 years to full capacity by 2032; two 10-year extension options tied to NRC nuclear license renewals; expected $18 billion revenue over contract life (Talen projection)
Status: Phase 1 operational (300 MW, 300,000 sq ft, early 2023); AWS expansion underway targeting 960 MW scaling; power ramp-up in 120 MW increments starting 2025; full buildout expected 2032
Investment: AWS acquisition $650 million; AWS Pennsylvania commitment ~$20 billion; Cumulus original investment by Talen recovered in sale

Timeline: July 2021 Talen announced Cumulus project; 2021 construction began; early 2023 Phase 1 operational; March 2024 AWS acquired Cumulus for $650M; 2025 power ramp-up begins; 2025 TeraWulf adds 50 MW; June 2025 AWS-Talen 1.92 GW PPA signed; 2032 full 960 MW AWS buildout completion

Strategic Innovations: AWS and Talen exploring SMR construction in Pennsylvania; exploring Susquehanna plant uprates; regional delivery capability beyond campus; front-of-meter PPA framework bypasses FERC restrictions on co-located loads

Regulatory & Technical: FERC rejected initial co-location interconnection agreement in Nov 2024; new front-of-meter framework avoids FERC approval requirement; PPL Electric Utilities handles transmission and delivery; Talen acts as retail power supplier; NRC license renewal tied to extension options

Competitive Advantages: 100% nuclear carbon-free power; 24/7 baseload generation; fixed-price economics; 2,520 MW plant capacity; Northeast Pennsylvania PJM/East Coast positioning; two-reactor redundancy
Cryptomining Integration: TeraWulf Nautilus facility existing and co-located; 25 MW original capacity doubling to 50 MW by 2025; separate from AWS operations; Talen retains interest

Community & Economic Impact: Salem Township and Luzerne County economic benefit; significant construction and operations workforce; nuclear facility operation stabilization; regional Pennsylvania power economy; $18 billion Talen revenue over PPA lifetime
Future Expansion Plans: SMR construction exploration; Susquehanna uprates; net-new energy to PJM grid; regional delivery capability; potential additional AWS facilities in Pennsylvania

Notes: AWS Susquehanna Nuclear Campus represents revolutionary nuclear-powered hyperscale data center. $650M Cumulus purchase in March 2024. 960 MW maximum capacity planned with 120 MW incremental ramps starting 2025 and full 2032 target. 1.92 GW PPA signed June 2025 through 2042. 100% carbon-free nuclear baseload power. Innovative front-of-meter PPA structure. Phase 1 operational at 300 MW and 300,000 sq ft. Strategic AWS $20B Pennsylvania commitment anchored by Susquehanna facility. SMR and uprate exploration indicate long-term vision. Model for nuclear-powered AI infrastructure.

currentProjectDetails: Location: Vermont State Legislature (statewide regulatory framework)
Type: Legislation / Regulatory Framework
Status: Passed by Legislature (May 2026)
Vote: Nearly unanimous (Senate and House)
Effective Date: Post-signature (2026+)
Regulatory Scope: Statewide data center development standards

Power Threshold: Applies to facilities requiring 20+ megawatts (MW); targets hyperscale/large-scale data centers; excludes smaller regional colocation facilities such as FirstLight Burlington (~2-5 MW)

Key Mandate 1 - Large Load Service Equity Contract: Developers must sign binding agreements protecting local utility ratepayers from subsidy burden, ensuring grid expansions do not increase residential utility costs, and establishing cost-sharing/equity mechanisms

Key Mandate 2 - Virtual Power Plants Requirement: Facilities must integrate virtual power plant (VPP) technology, enable demand response participation, support grid stabilization, and provide load flexibility during peak periods
Key Mandate 3 - Battery Storage Integration: Mandatory battery energy storage systems for peak load management, grid resilience, and backup power capacity

Key Mandate 4 - Closed-Loop Water Systems: Strict water conservation requirements, closed-loop cooling, zero external discharge, municipal water independence, and environmental protection to reduce contamination risk
Legislative Leadership: Vermont Senate and House bipartisan support; nearly unanimous passage in May 2026; strong community and environmental coalition backing; proactive regulatory positioning

Strategic Purpose: Establish guardrails before hyperscalers arrive, protect ratepayer interests, mandate renewable and sustainable infrastructure, prevent environmental/water contamination, and ensure equitable cost allocation
Unique Model: Preemptive legislation rather than reactive opposition; comprehensive sustainability requirements; grid protection focus; community equity emphasis

Notes: Vermont H. 727 Sustainable Data Centers Act represents a unique preemptive regulatory approach. Passed May 2026 with nearly unanimous support, it uses a 20+ MW threshold to target hyperscale facilities and mandates ratepayer protections, virtual power plants, battery storage, and closed-loop water systems before large-scale data center development reaches the state.

currentProjects.title: State Senate Bill 205 (Temporary Moratorium)
details: Location: Vermont State Senate (statewide legislative proposal)
Type: Legislation / Temporary Moratorium
Status: Pending in Committee (Senate Finance Committee)
Introduced by: Senator Becca White + coalition of lawmakers
Moratorium Duration: Four years (temporary)
Scope: All large-scale AI data centers
Moratorium Parameters: Blocks all large-scale AI data center siting during the environmental/economic study phase; temporary measure, not permanent ban; safety valve if H. 727 proves inadequate

Purpose: Legal mechanism to halt siting during assessment, enable comprehensive environmental and economic impact analysis, provide time for community consultation, and create implementation runway for H. 727
Complementary to H. 727: Works alongside the Sustainable Data Centers Act as a backup if the regulatory framework is insufficient; buys time for impact assessment and deliberate decision-making

Environmental/Economic Assessment: Exhaustive environmental impact report, economic cost-benefit analysis, community benefit planning, infrastructure capacity evaluation, and water/power grid stress testing
Legislative Status: Introduced by Senator White and coalition; pending Senate Finance Committee review; companion to H. 727 regulatory framework
Strategic Approach: Dual-track strategy combining H. 727 proactive regulation with S. 205 temporal safeguard, preventing race-to-the-bottom dynamics

Notes: Vermont S. 205 represents a four-year temporary moratorium on large-scale AI data centers. It complements H. 727 by blocking rushed siting during a comprehensive environmental and economic assessment period.

currentProjects.title: Federal Guardrail Proposals (Bernie Sanders / AOC Bill)
details: Location: United States Congress (federal legislative initiative)
Type: Federal Legislation / National Moratorium Proposal
Status: Introduced in Congress
Leadership: Senator Bernie Sanders (Vermont Independent) + Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-NY)
Scope: National AI data center moratorium across the United States
Proposal Parameters: Blocks new large-scale computing permits until strict national safeguards are established; applies across all states and regions
Core Mandate 1 - Electricity Rate Protection: Prevents corporate AI infrastructure from spiking rates for residential and small business customers and blocks utility cost externalization
Core Mandate 2 - Climate Goal Alignment: Ensures data centers do not undermine regional climate goals through renewable energy requirements, carbon accounting standards, and climate impact assessment

Core Mandate 3 - National Safeguards Framework: Establishes federal guardrails, minimum sustainability standards, water protection requirements, grid stability mandates, and community benefit standards

Strategic Vision: Federal regulatory ceiling to prevent state-level race-to-bottom dynamics, protect vulnerable communities, ensure climate integrity, protect electricity affordability, and establish national equity standards
Political Positioning: Sanders brings Vermont climate/equity leadership; AOC brings environmental justice focus; growing state-level momentum supports the national proposal
Precedent Setting: Federal action on AI infrastructure as a novel policy domain; national approach rather than state-by-state fragmentation; potential international governance model

Notes: The Sanders/AOC federal AI data center moratorium proposal would block new large-scale computing permits until national safeguards are established, including electricity rate protection, climate alignment, and community benefit standards.

currentProjects.title: Local Advocacy Campaigns Against Regional Siting
details: Location: Vermont (multiple communities, statewide grassroots)
Type: Community Organizing / Advocacy
Status: Active grassroots organizing
Key Organizations: Vermont Natural Resources Council (VNRC), environmental coalitions, and community groups
Scope: Statewide opposition and oversight

Advocacy Focus 1 - Act 250 Land-Use Protection: Vermont foundational 1970 land-use statute, strict environmental review standards, community veto authority, historic preservation of Vermont character, and prevention of commercial/industrial sprawl

Advocacy Focus 2 - Rural Land Preservation: Preventing large-footprint industrial development, protecting agricultural and forest land conversion, maintaining rural character, preventing corporate land consolidation, and preserving property tax base diversity

Advocacy Focus 3 - Ridgeline Protection: Preventing substations, transmission lines, cooling towers, and other infrastructure on ridgelines; scenic viewshed preservation; mountain landscape protection

Advocacy Focus 4 - Water Basin Protection: Preventing PFAS contamination, protecting municipal water supplies, preventing groundwater depletion, advocating for closed-loop water systems, and defending drinking water sources
VNRC Leadership: Vermont Natural Resources Council mobilization, environmental coalition coordination, community group activation, strict oversight advocacy, and Act 250 enforcement focus
Grassroots Strategy: Town meetings, select boards, coalition building, legal challenge preparation, public education campaigns, media engagement, and petitions as needed
Legal Mechanisms: Act 250 review process, select board approval requirements, environmental impact assessment challenges, water/conservation law enforcement, and regulatory agency comment periods

Opposition Arguments: Rural character preservation, water contamination risks, grid strain and utility cost impacts, land-use sprawl prevention, community self-determination, and climate impact concerns

Political Leverage: Vermont culture of local control and Town Meeting tradition, strong environmental movement legacy, agricultural/forestry community alignment, bipartisan land-use preservation support, and Act 250 legal/political entrenchment

Notes: Vermont local advocacy campaigns provide the grassroots layer to H. 727 and S. 205, using Act 250, select board engagement, environmental review, and community organizing to scrutinize or oppose large data center siting.

largestFacilityDetails: Location: Mount Pleasant, Racine County, Wisconsin (90th Street and Highway KR, formerly Foxconn site)
Developer: Microsoft Cloud
Status: Operational (Fairwater 1 brought online early 2026, ahead of schedule)
Investment: Fairwater 1 $3.3 billion; Fairwater 2 planned $4 billion; total $7.3+ billion Wisconsin commitment
Land: 315 acres total (multiphase development)
Total space: Fairwater 1 1.2 million sq ft; Fairwater 2 plus expansion includes 15 additional data centers approved January 2026; combined planned 10+ million sq ft
Configuration: Superfactory for frontier AI model training and operations
Tenancy: Microsoft internal (Azure cloud, OpenAI operations, frontier AI)
Power capacity: 555 MW operational (Fairwater 1)
GPU Infrastructure: Hundreds of thousands of NVIDIA GPUs, NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs, GB200 NVL72 high-density systems, and claimed 10x performance of today's fastest supercomputers

Timeline: March 2023 project announced; 2023-2024 Phase 1 construction; July 1, 2026 Phase 1 target met/exceeded; early 2026 Fairwater 1 operational; 2026+ Fairwater 2 construction; January 2026 approval for 15 additional data centers

Construction Scale: 46.6 miles of deep foundation piles, 26.5 million pounds of structural steel, 120 miles of medium-voltage underground cable, 72.6 miles of mechanical piping, and massive infrastructure buildout

15 Additional Data Centers: Approved January 2026; 9 southern-site data centers around 580,000 sq ft each; 6 northern-site data centers around 568,000 sq ft each; located on former Foxconn-slated land; split across two areas northwest of current site; roughly 8.8 million sq ft additional capacity

Water Management: 2.8 million gallons of Lake Michigan water per year for Fairwater 1, equivalent to 4 Olympic-sized swimming pools; Great Lakes-St. Lawrence River Basin Water Resources Compact compliance; Racine diversion approval of 7 million gallons per day; water returned through Racine Wastewater Treatment Plant; closed-loop liquid cooling; Mount Pleasant straddling community exception
Cooling Technology: Closed-loop liquid cooling, reduced-latency architecture, flat network design for high-density GPU clusters, and thermal efficiency optimization

Employment: 375 people hired currently; 500 full-time employees for Fairwater 1 operations; 800 employees after Fairwater 2 completion; hundreds more possible with 15-data-center expansion; multiyear union construction jobs; salaries in the $75,000-$200,000+ range

Education & Workforce Development: Wisconsin first Datacenter Academy at Gateway Technical College, AI Co-Innovation Lab at UW-Milwaukee, manufacturing AI solutions focus, local partnerships with Regal Rexnord, Renaissant, and BW Converting, and broadband expansion serving 9,300 rural residents

Community Benefits: Ahead-of-schedule property value growth targets, broadband expansion for 1,200 homes/businesses in Sturtevant, union construction employment, long-term tech operations careers, local manufacturer AI partnerships, and technology education initiatives

Strategic Significance: Microsoft describes Fairwater as the world most powerful AI data center; Superfactory concept for frontier AI training/operations; hundreds of thousands of NVIDIA Blackwell/GB200 GPUs; claimed 10x supercomputer performance; frontier AI model training focus; OpenAI operations support

CEO Personal Connection: Microsoft Vice Chair/President Brad Smith grew up in Mount Pleasant, with childhood home and school/newspaper delivery roots in the community influencing personal and strategic commitment to Wisconsin

Competitive Advantages: World-leading AI data center designation, hundreds of thousands of GPUs, closed-loop liquid cooling, Great Lakes water access, 315+ acre expandable site, Midwest location 30 miles south of Milwaukee and 60 miles north of Chicago, and Foxconn site conversion

Future Expansion Vision: 15 additional approved data centers, potential for hundreds more, multi-decade buildout runway, Midwest regional AI infrastructure anchor, OpenAI partnership scaling, and frontier AI model training expansion

Regional Context: Alliant Energy service territory, 12+ other data center projects in Alliant area, Wisconsin emerging as major AI infrastructure hub, Midwest power grid modernization focus, and regional economic transformation

Notes: Microsoft Fairwater represents a world-leading AI data center campus in Mount Pleasant. Fairwater 1 is operational ahead of schedule with 555 MW capacity, 1.2M sq ft, hundreds of thousands of NVIDIA Blackwell/GB200 GPUs, and a $7.3B+ Wisconsin commitment. The campus converts former Foxconn land into a frontier AI Superfactory supporting Azure and OpenAI operations.

Power access is a primary constraint, with nuclear and renewable agreements appearing in major projects.
Hyperscalers including Google, Amazon, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI, Oracle, and SoftBank are driving expansion.
Growth is shifting beyond Virginia and California toward Texas, Ohio, Pennsylvania, Wisconsin, Indiana, Iowa, Mississippi, and North Carolina.
Land availability, power access, and tax incentives are major site-selection drivers.
largestFacilityDetails: Location: North Range Business Park, west of Cheyenne, Laramie County, Wyoming (Interstate 25/I-80 interchange near Roundtop Road)
Acquisition date: 2012 (original construction began)
Purchase price: Not disclosed
Seller: Not disclosed
Original build: 2012 (initial facility operational)
Land: Original + 120-acre expansion (2015 announced), 923.93 acres annexed (2024)
Total space: Estimated 400,000+ sq ft (multiple buildings, exact not disclosed)
Configuration: Multi-building Azure cloud infrastructure campus
Tenancy: Microsoft internal (Azure cloud services)
Power capacity: 200 MW (current largest in Cheyenne market)
Power source: 100% wind-powered (since 2016)
59 MW from Happy Jack and Silver Sage wind projects (Black Hills Energy)
178 MW from Bloom Wind project in Kansas (Allianz Risk Transfer)
Combined wind capacity: 237+ MW (exceeds facility needs)
Status: Operational since 2012, continuously expanding (under active construction as of 2025-2026)
Employment: 50+ full-time jobs (originally promised 25, already exceeded)
Investment: $750 million+ to date
Investment timeline: 2012 initial construction; 2014 $247 million expansion; 2015 $200+ million expansion announced with 120 acres added; 2018-2026 continuous expansion work (delayed, then resumed)
Infrastructure: Natural gas backup generators (vs. diesel); ITPACs (Integrated Modular Containers) deployment; water/sewer infrastructure expansion (2024-2026); road infrastructure improvements

Regional Context: Part of Cheyenne market with 21 data centers and 8 providers; Microsoft operates 11 facilities in Cheyenne region; largest facility in Cheyenne market; adjacent to NCAR-Wyoming Supercomputing Center

Renewable Energy Innovation: 100% wind-powered since 2016; natural gas backup instead of diesel; wind contracts with Kansas and Wyoming providers; over-provisioned renewable capacity (237 MW for 200 MW facility)

Expansion Timeline: 2015 $200M expansion announced with 18-month delay due to server efficiency upgrades; 2017-2018 expansion resumed; 2024 city annexation of 923.93 acres; 2025 two new facilities announced; 2026 ongoing active construction

Community Investment: TechSpark Wyoming region development; water commitment to replenish more water than consumed; job creation; infrastructure funding for water, sewer, and roads; tax base contribution to schools, hospitals, and libraries

Notes: Microsoft's flagship Wyoming data center since 2012. North Range Business Park anchor tenant. 200 MW operational capacity (largest in Cheyenne market). 100% wind-powered since 2016 (pioneering renewable commitment). $750M+ cumulative investment. Continuous expansion with 923.93 acres recently annexed for future growth. Innovative use of natural gas backup vs. diesel. Over-provisioned wind contracts (237 MW for 200 MW facility). Adjacent to NCAR supercomputing center (unique co-location of cloud + HPC infrastructure). Two additional facilities announced 2025 (Cheyenne Business Parkway, Bison Business Park) indicating major regional expansion beyond North Range campus.
